title: How to Cure Bacon at Home
excerpt: Home-cured bacon is in a different league from anything you'll find at a grocery store. Here's how to do it safely using pink curing salt.

sections.heading: Why Cure Your Own Bacon?
sections.body:
Store-bought bacon is fine. Home-cured bacon is extraordinary. When you cure your own, you control the salt level, the smoke, the thickness of the slice, and most importantly — the quality of the pork. The result is something deeply savory, with a clean, complex flavor that commercial bacon rarely achieves.

It's also simpler than most people think. The main ingredient beyond the pork belly is pink curing salt — and once you understand how to use it correctly, the process is mostly just waiting.

sections.heading: What Is Pink Curing Salt?
sections.body:
Pink curing salt (also called Prague Powder #1 or Instacure #1) is a mixture of regular salt and sodium nitrite (6.25%). The pink color is added intentionally to distinguish it from regular table salt — they should never be confused.

Sodium nitrite serves two critical functions: it inhibits the growth of Clostridium botulinum (the bacterium that causes botulism), and it gives cured meats their characteristic pink color and savory flavor. Without it, what you're making is salt-packed pork — not bacon.

sections.heading: How Much Pink Curing Salt to Use
sections.body:
This is the most important part of home curing, and the rule is simple: use exactly 1 teaspoon (about 6.25g) of Prague Powder #1 per 5 pounds of meat. Do not eyeball this. Do not exceed it.

The sodium nitrite in pink curing salt is effective at very small concentrations. Too little, and the cure won't be safe. Too much, and the meat will taste bitter and overly salty. Use a kitchen scale and measure precisely.

sections.heading: The Basic Cure Method
sections.body:
For a 3–5 lb pork belly, combine: 1 teaspoon Crave Pink Curing Salt #1, 2 tablespoons kosher salt, 2 tablespoons brown sugar, and optional aromatics like black pepper, garlic powder, or smoked paprika.

Rub the cure mix thoroughly over all surfaces of the pork belly. Place in a zip-lock bag or covered container in the refrigerator. Turn and redistribute the cure daily. Cure time for bacon is typically 7 days — the meat should feel firm all the way through when done.

sections.heading: After Curing: Smoking or Roasting
sections.body:
Once cured, rinse the pork belly under cold water and pat dry. From here you can smoke it low and slow (185°F for 3–4 hours over applewood or hickory) or roast it in a 200°F oven until it reaches an internal temperature of 150°F.

After cooking, rest it completely before slicing. A partially frozen belly slices much more cleanly if you're going for thin strips. Slice, cook, and enjoy — it will be the best bacon you've ever had.

title: What Is Clear Jel and Why Do Canners Swear By It?
excerpt: Clear Jel is the only starch thickener approved by the USDA for home canning. Here's why it outperforms cornstarch and how to use it correctly.

sections.heading: The Problem with Cornstarch in Canning
sections.body:
If you've ever tried to use cornstarch in a home-canned pie filling, you've probably noticed something go wrong: the filling breaks down, turns watery, and sometimes doesn't set at all. This isn't user error — cornstarch simply cannot withstand the heat and acid conditions of the canning process.

During water bath or pressure canning, temperatures inside the jar exceed what cornstarch can handle. The starch granules break apart, losing their thickening power. What looked perfect going in comes out thin and separated.

sections.heading: What Makes Clear Jel Different
sections.body:
Clear Jel is a modified food starch — specifically a waxy maize starch that has been chemically modified to withstand high heat, freezing, and acidic environments without breaking down. It was developed for exactly this use case.

The USDA's National Center for Home Food Preservation recommends Clear Jel as the only approved thickener for canned pie fillings. No other starch — not cornstarch, not flour, not arrowroot — has been tested and approved for this purpose.

sections.heading: Cook Type vs. Instant Clear Jel
sections.body:
There are two types: cook type and instant. Cook type must be heated to thicken — it's what you want for canning and stovetop pie fillings. Instant Clear Jel thickens without heat, making it useful for cold applications like no-bake fillings.

Crave Seasonings carries cook type Clear Jel, which is ideal for canning, baked pies, and any cooked fruit filling. Do not substitute one for the other in recipes — the thickening behavior is significantly different.

sections.heading: How Much Clear Jel to Use
sections.body:
For most canned fruit pie fillings the standard ratio is 7 tablespoons per quart of filling for peaches, apples, and berries, and 5–6 tablespoons for more naturally thick fruits like cherries.

Always mix Clear Jel with sugar before adding to fruit to prevent lumping. Bring the mixture to a full rolling boil and cook for 1 minute before filling jars. The filling will look almost too thick at this stage — it thins slightly during processing and sets back up as it cools.

sections.heading: Beyond Canning: Other Uses
sections.body: Clear Jel's stability makes it excellent for commercial-style gravies, fruit sauces, and glazes that need to hold up on a buffet or reheat cleanly. It produces a clear, glossy finish that cornstarch can't match. For bakers who sell at markets or do large-volume production, it's a professional-grade tool that makes a visible difference.

title: Xanthan Gum 101: The Keto and Gluten-Free Baker's Secret Weapon
excerpt: A tiny amount of xanthan gum can transform gluten-free and keto baked goods from crumbly to perfectly structured. Here's everything you need to know.

sections.heading: What Is Xanthan Gum?
sections.body:
Xanthan gum is a polysaccharide produced by fermenting glucose with a bacterium called Xanthomonas campestris. Despite the scientific-sounding name, it's a completely food-safe ingredient found in everything from salad dressings to ice cream to gluten-free bread.

In practical terms, xanthan gum acts as a binder and thickener. It holds things together that would otherwise fall apart, and it creates viscosity in liquids even at very small concentrations.

sections.heading: Why Gluten-Free Baking Needs It
sections.body:
Gluten is the protein structure in wheat that gives bread its chew and structure. When you remove gluten, baked goods often become crumbly, dry, and structureless. They don't rise well, don't hold their shape, and lack the satisfying texture of the real thing.

Xanthan gum mimics some of what gluten does — it binds ingredients together, traps air bubbles during baking, and creates a more elastic dough. The result: gluten-free baked goods that actually hold together and have real texture.

sections.heading: How Much to Use
sections.body:
The key with xanthan gum is restraint. Too little and you won't notice the difference. Too much and your baked goods will have a gummy texture that's worse than none at all.

General guidelines: cakes and muffins — 1/4 tsp per cup of gluten-free flour; cookies — 1/2 tsp per cup; bread — 1/2 to 1 tsp per cup; pizza dough — 1 tsp per cup; sauces and dressings — 1/8 to 1/4 tsp per cup of liquid. Always start at the lower end and adjust.

sections.heading: Tips for Working with It
sections.body:
Xanthan gum clumps when it hits liquid. Always mix it with your dry ingredients before adding any liquid. If adding to a sauce or dressing, whisk it into oil first, then add the liquid gradually while stirring.

For keto baking with almond or coconut flour, xanthan gum is particularly important because these flours have almost no binding properties on their own. A small amount goes a long way toward making keto baked goods that don't fall apart when you pick them up.

title: The Two Secrets to Bakery-Quality Soft Pretzels
excerpt: After all the recipe videos and blog posts, it really comes down to two things: a proper baking soda bath and the right pretzel salt. Here's why both matter.

sections.heading: Why Most Homemade Pretzels Fall Short
sections.body:
Homemade soft pretzels are often fine. They taste good — soft, bready, a little salty. But they rarely have that specific quality of a real pretzel-shop pretzel: the deep brown color, the slightly chewy-crisp exterior, the crust that has structural integrity, and the salt that crunches rather than dissolves.

The gap usually comes down to two things most home bakers get wrong: the alkaline bath and the salt.

sections.heading: The Baking Soda Bath
sections.body:
Traditional pretzels are dipped in a lye solution before baking. Lye is highly caustic and not practical for most home kitchens. The common substitute is a baking soda bath — much milder but still effective.

The trick most home bakers miss: baking the baking soda before dissolving it. Spread baking soda on a sheet pan and bake at 250°F for one hour. This converts it to sodium carbonate, which is more alkaline and produces much better browning and flavor. Use 2/3 cup per 10 cups of water, and dip each pretzel for 30 seconds before it goes in the oven.

sections.heading: Why Pretzel Salt Matters
sections.body:
Most home recipes list "coarse salt" and people grab whatever is in the cabinet — usually kosher salt, sometimes table salt. The result is salt that dissolves into the pretzel, leaves wet patches, or turns brown and discolored in the oven.

Coarse pretzel salt is formulated specifically for this application. Large, flat crystals don't absorb moisture from the dough. It's heat-stable up to 400°F, staying bright white after baking. And the crystal shape gives it that distinctive, satisfying crunch that makes you reach for another pretzel.

sections.heading: Getting the Application Right
sections.body:
Apply pretzel salt immediately after the baking soda bath, before the surface dries. Press gently so the crystals stick. Use more than you think you need — pretzels from professional shops are generously salted, not lightly dusted. The large crystals mean a heavy hand doesn't make them taste overwhelmingly salty.

Bake at 450°F and don't open the oven early. The high heat and the alkaline surface are what create that glossy, deep-brown exterior that separates a real pretzel from a bread roll in the shape of one.
